text:
MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — POST-TREATMENT RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female with a known history of p16-positive (HPV-associated) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging; stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation — intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) via volumetric modulated arc therapy to 70 Gy in 35 fractions at the primary site and positive right level IIA node, with concurrent weekly Platinol 40 mg/m2 — approximately ten weeks prior to this examination. This study is obtained for anatomic post-treatment restaging and correlation with a concurrent restaging PET-CT, which demonstrated complete metabolic response with no residual FDG-avid disease.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the neck was performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner utilizing a dedicated head-and-neck surface coil array. Sequences obtained include: axial and coronal T1-weighted spin echo (pre-contrast), axial fat-suppressed T2-weighted fast spin echo (STIR), axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping (b-values 0, 500, 1000 s/mm2), and axial, sagittal, and coronal T1-weighted fat-saturated sequences acquired following intravenous administration of gadobutrol (Gadavist) 0.1 mmol/kg, injected via right antecubital access at 2 mL/s. No contraindications to gadolinium. No prior allergic reactions reported. Comparison is made to the pre-treatment baseline MRI of the neck performed at the time of initial staging.


FINDINGS:

Primary Site — Right Base of Tongue:
On the pre-treatment baseline study, a 3.2 x 2.8 cm exophytic, avidly enhancing soft tissue mass was centered at the right base of tongue, extending toward the right tonsillar fossa but with preserved vallecula and pre-epiglottic fat.

On the current examination, there has been marked interval reduction in the right base of tongue soft tissue abnormality. In the region of the known primary tumor, there is now a small residual area of mucosal thickening and submucosal signal abnormality measuring approximately 0.9 x 0.7 cm in the axial plane (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm), representing a dramatic reduction in bulk from the pre-treatment baseline. This residual focus demonstrates heterogeneously reduced T2 signal intensity compared to the original mass, with low-signal fibrous stranding on T2-weighted sequences. On post-contrast imaging, there is faint, mildly heterogeneous enhancement within this residual focus, substantially diminished in degree and extent compared to the pre-treatment avid enhancement pattern. On DWI, there is no convincing restricted diffusion; ADC values within this residual area are elevated (approximately 1.3 x 10^-3 mm2/s), favoring treatment-related fibrosis over viable tumor, though a small component of residual microscopic disease cannot be entirely excluded by imaging alone.

The right tonsillar fossa: no discrete enhancing soft tissue mass. Post-radiation mucosal irregularity noted, without a definable nodule or mass. The glossotonsillar sulcus on the right appears intact with fibrous remodeling. No extension to the vallecula. The pre-epiglottic fat plane is preserved and shows no infiltrating soft tissue.

There is diffuse post-radiation signal change throughout the oropharyngeal soft tissues bilaterally: increased T2 signal and slight mucosal thickening involving the pharyngeal walls and soft palate mucosa, consistent with post-radiation inflammatory and edematous changes. The parapharyngeal fat shows mild bilateral symmetric edematous change, greater on the right — expected in the setting of recent IMRT.

Mandible and Skull Base:
No cortical erosion, marrow infiltration, or periosteal reaction within the mandible, floor of mouth, or skull base. The mandibular cortex is intact. The pterygopalatine fossa and infratemporal fossa are clear of tumor extension. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
Right Level IIA (previously positive, 2.1 x 1.8 cm, with internal necrosis and peripheral rim enhancement): The previously abnormal right level IIA lymph node has markedly decreased in size and now measures 0.8 x 0.6 cm. The internal necrotic area previously identified is no longer present. The node demonstrates homogeneous, low-to-intermediate T2 signal without internal fluid signal, and shows no pathologic enhancement on post-contrast sequences — the enhancement pattern is symmetric with and indistinguishable from adjacent small reactive nodes. The ADC value within this node is elevated (approximately 1.5 x 10^-3 mm2/s), consistent with fibrotic replacement rather than viable metastatic tissue. Overall, the morphologic appearance is consistent with a treated, fibrotic lymph node without imaging evidence of residual viable metastatic adenopathy.

No new pathologic lymphadenopathy is identified in the right neck at levels I through V. No evidence of extracapsular soft tissue extension at any nodal station. Contralateral (left) neck: several small lymph nodes are visible at level II and III bilaterally, none meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement, with normal fatty hila and no abnormal enhancement. No retropharyngeal adenopathy. No posterior cervical space adenopathy. No supraclavicular or low cervical level VI adenopathy.

Salivary Glands:
The right parotid gland demonstrates slightly reduced volume compared to the pre-treatment baseline, with mildly heterogeneous T1 signal and reduced fat replacement, consistent with early radiation-induced parenchymal changes. The left parotid gland is relatively preserved in volume. Both submandibular glands are mildly edematous in signal but without focal lesion. No obstructing calculi or ductal dilatation identified.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is within the lower field of view. It appears mildly heterogeneous in echotexture on T2-weighted sequences but without discrete focal nodule or mass. No suspicious enhancement. Clinical correlation with thyroid function tests and ultrasound is noted to be ongoing given prior irradiation of the lower neck.

Vascular Structures:
The bilateral carotid arteries and internal jugular veins are patent and demonstrate normal flow voids. No vascular encasement or displacement by soft tissue. No periarterial soft tissue thickening.

Airway:
The oropharyngeal airway is patent. No soft tissue encroachment from the base of tongue region. The epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds appear unremarkable; no supraglottic soft tissue mass.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
Diffuse subcutaneous edema and mild skin thickening are noted throughout the right neck soft tissues within the prior radiation field, particularly along the right lateral and posterior neck — consistent with post-radiation soft tissue change and skin thickening. No organized fluid collection, abscess, or drainable hematoma. No evidence of osteoradionecrosis or soft tissue necrosis.

Cranial Base and Imaged Intracranial Structures:
The imaged portions of the posterior fossa and skull base are unremarkable. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No calvarial lytic lesion within the field of view.

Perineural Spread:
No imaging evidence of perineural tumor spread along the lingual nerve, inferior alveolar nerve, or hypoglossal nerve. Neural foramina appear patent and of normal caliber.


IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval reduction of the right base of tongue primary tumor, now represented by a small residual submucosal focus measuring 0.9 x 0.7 cm (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm). The markedly diminished enhancement and elevated ADC values within this residual focus are most consistent with post-treatment fibrosis and scar tissue; however, complete exclusion of microscopic residual tumor by MRI alone is limited. Clinical and endoscopic correlation is recommended.

2. Dramatic response at the previously FDG-avid right level IIA metastatic lymph node, now substantially reduced to 0.8 cm, with complete resolution of internal necrosis and normalization of enhancement characteristics. Appearance is most consistent with fibrotic replacement; no imaging features of residual viable nodal metastasis.

3. No new cervical, retropharyngeal, or contralateral cervical adenopathy identified.

4. No distant disease within the field of view. No evidence of bone marrow involvement of the mandible or skull base.

5. Diffuse post-radiation inflammatory soft tissue and mucosal changes throughout the right oropharynx and neck, appearing expected for approximately ten weeks following completion of high-dose IMRT.

6. Early radiation-induced changes in the right parotid gland; ongoing salivary function monitoring warranted.

7. These findings are concordant with the concurrent restaging PET-CT demonstrating complete metabolic response. Taken together, the overall picture is highly favorable and consistent with a near-complete to complete radiographic response to definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma.
